audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Abimelech kingship, Jotham fable, Shechem conflict, and Thebez death narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Abimelech's Shechem appeal, hired reckless men, slaughter of seventy brothers, Jotham's tree fable, three-year rule, evil spirit, Gaal revolt, Shechem massacre, tower burning, Thebez millstone death, armor-bearer suicide request, and bloodguilt return theology.

verses.restored: Abimelech son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem, to brothers of his mother, and spoke to them and to all clan of house of his mother's father, saying,

Speak, please, in ears of all lords of Shechem: Which is better for you, that seventy men rule over you, all sons of Jerubbaal, or that one man rule over you? Remember that I am your bone and your flesh.
Brothers of his mother spoke about him in ears of all lords of Shechem all these words, and their heart inclined after Abimelech, for they said, He is our brother.
They gave him seventy pieces of silver from house of Baal-berith; with them Abimelech hired empty and reckless men, and they went after him.
He came to house of his father at Ophrah and killed his brothers, sons of Jerubbaal, seventy men upon one stone. But Jotham youngest son of Jerubbaal remained, for he hid.
All lords of Shechem and all Beth-millo gathered and went and made Abimelech king by oak of pillar that was in Shechem.
They told Jotham, and he went and stood at top of Mount Gerizim and lifted his voice and called and said to them: Listen to me, lords of Shechem, so Divine may listen to you.
The trees surely went to anoint over themselves a king. They said to olive tree, Reign over us.
Olive tree said to them, Shall I cease my fatness/oil, by which Divine and men are honored, and go sway over trees?
The trees said to fig tree, You come, reign over us.
Fig tree said to them, Shall I cease my sweetness and my good fruit and go sway over trees?
The trees said to vine, You come, reign over us.
Vine said to them, Shall I cease my new wine, which gladdens Divine and men, and go sway over trees?
All trees said to bramble, You come, reign over us.
Bramble said to trees, If in faithfulness you are anointing me king over you, come take refuge in my shade; but if not, let fire go out from bramble and consume cedars of Lebanon.
Now therefore, if in faithfulness and wholeness you acted when you made Abimelech king, and if you did good with Jerubbaal and his house, and if according to deserving of his hands you did to him—
for my father fought for you and cast his soul/life in front and rescued you from hand of Midian,
but you rose against house of my father today and killed his sons, seventy men upon one stone, and made Abimelech, son of his slave woman, king over lords of Shechem because he is your brother—
if in faithfulness and wholeness you acted with Jerubbaal and his house this day, rejoice in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you.
But if not, let fire go out from Abimelech and consume lords of Shechem and Beth-millo, and let fire go out from lords of Shechem and Beth-millo and consume Abimelech.
Jotham fled and escaped and went to Beer; he dwelt there away from Abimelech his brother.
Abimelech ruled over Israel three years.
Divine sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and lords of Shechem, and lords of Shechem acted treacherously against Abimelech,

so violence done to seventy sons of Jerubbaal might come, and their blood be placed upon Abimelech their brother who killed them and upon lords of Shechem who strengthened his hands to kill his brothers.
Lords of Shechem set ambushers against him on tops of mountains, and they robbed everyone who passed by them on road; it was told to Abimelech.
Gaal son of Ebed came with his brothers and crossed into Shechem, and lords of Shechem trusted in him.
They went out to field, gathered their vineyards, trod grapes, and made praise-songs/revelry; they came into house of their god, ate and drank, and cursed Abimelech.

Gaal son of Ebed said, Who is Abimelech, and who is Shechem, that we should serve him? Is he not son of Jerubbaal, and Zebul his officer? Serve men of Hamor father of Shechem; why should we serve him?
Would that this people were in my hand! Then I would remove Abimelech. He said to Abimelech, Increase your army and come out.
Zebul ruler of city heard words of Gaal son of Ebed, and his anger burned.
He sent messengers to Abimelech in deceit/Tormah, saying, Look, Gaal son of Ebed and his brothers are coming to Shechem, and look, they are stirring city against you.
Now rise by night, you and people with you, and lie in ambush in field.
It shall be in morning, when sun rises, rise early and rush upon city. Look, when he and people with him come out to you, you shall do to him as your hand finds.
Abimelech and all people with him rose by night and lay in ambush against Shechem, four companies.
Gaal son of Ebed went out and stood at entrance of gate of city, and Abimelech and people with him rose from ambush.
Gaal saw people and said to Zebul, Look, people are coming down from tops of mountains. Zebul said to him, Shadow of mountains you are seeing as men.
Gaal spoke again and said, Look, people are coming down from navel of land, and one company is coming from way of Diviners' Oak.
Zebul said to him, Where now is your mouth, you who said, Who is Abimelech that we should serve him? Is this not people you despised? Go out now and fight with them.
Gaal went out before lords of Shechem and fought with Abimelech.
Abimelech pursued him, and he fled before him; many fell slain as far as entrance of gate.
Abimelech dwelt at Arumah, and Zebul drove out Gaal and his brothers from dwelling in Shechem.
It was next day that people went out to field, and they told Abimelech.
He took people, divided them into three companies, and lay in ambush in field. He looked, and behold, people were coming out from city; he rose against them and struck them.
Abimelech and companies with him rushed and stood at entrance of gate of city, while two companies rushed upon all who were in field and struck them.
Abimelech fought against city all that day, captured city, killed people in it, tore down city, and sowed it with salt.
All lords of tower of Shechem heard and entered stronghold of house of El-berith.
It was told to Abimelech that all lords of tower of Shechem had gathered.

Abimelech went up to Mount Zalmon, he and all people with him. Abimelech took axes in his hand, cut down a branch of trees, lifted it, put it on his shoulder, and said to people with him, What you have seen me do, hurry, do as I have done.

All people also cut each man his branch and went after Abimelech, set them against stronghold, and set stronghold on fire over them. All people of tower of Shechem also died, about a thousand men and women.
Abimelech went to Thebez, camped against Thebez, and captured it.
A strong tower was in midst of city, and there fled all men and women and all lords of city. They shut themselves in and went up on roof of tower.
Abimelech came to tower and fought against it; he drew near to entrance of tower to burn it with fire.
A certain woman threw an upper millstone upon Abimelech's head and crushed his skull.
He called quickly to young man, his armor-bearer, and said to him, Draw your sword and kill me, lest they say of me, A woman killed him. His young man thrust him through, and he died.
Men of Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, and they went each to his place.
Thus Divine returned evil of Abimelech that he did to his father by killing his seventy brothers.
And all evil of men of Shechem Divine returned on their head, and upon them came curse of Jotham son of Jerubbaal.

translation_notes: Abimelech builds power through kinship manipulation, temple funds, hired reckless men, and mass fratricide. This is anti-kingship narrative, not a legitimate accession story.
The phrase 'son of his slave woman' exposes status hierarchy and maternal shaming in Jotham's speech; the critique of Abimelech must not become contempt for enslaved or secondary-status mothers.
Jotham's fable contrasts fruitful trees that refuse domination with bramble kingship that offers false shade and real fire.
The evil spirit between Abimelech and Shechem describes destructive rupture and mutual treachery; it must not be weaponized against mentally ill or neurodivergent people.
Abimelech's violence escalates from brother-killing to civil terror, city destruction, salt-sowing, and burning a thousand men and women inside a tower.
The woman of Thebez kills Abimelech with an upper millstone, reversing his attempt to burn another tower full of civilians.
Abimelech's fear of being remembered as killed by a woman exposes misogynistic honor anxiety, not a valid shame.
The final verses frame the chapter as bloodguilt returning upon Abimelech and Shechem, but this should not justify cycles of revenge or civilian massacre.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 9 to justify monarchy by violence, family slaughter, mercenary politics, collective burning, ableist evil-spirit language, maternal-status shaming, misogynistic honor codes, or revenge cycles. Center Jotham's anti-tyrant warning: bramble power promises shade and produces fire.
